text: Hello, I'm sharing a acute cholecystitis. This was done on call.
Patient came in through the emergency room after about twenty-four hours of severe discomfort and workup confirming acute cholecystitis.
I chose to do the surgery robotically. I think that the options of laparoscopy and robotic are pretty equivalent other than perhaps surgeon preference.
And recently I have just been a lot more comfortable sitting at the console rather than standing at the bedside for these more challenging or expectedly challenging gallbladders.
The surgeon uses instruments to take down adhesions between the omentum and perinephric fat on the liver, then begins dissecting the gallbladder starting from the lateral side.
I found ICG to be very helpful in this case, like I do for most of my laparoscopic and robotic cholecystectomies.
There was nothing intrinsically unusual or different about this case compared to any other gallbladder operations.
The video shows a large stone lodged in the neck of the gallbladder, highlighting the steps and flow of the operation.
made the manipulation of the gallbladder for exposure a little bit more challenging, but otherwise the case went pretty smooth.
I don't usually have a scissors and a hook for a cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses scissors and a hook during the cholecystectomy, with the common bile duct visible under ICG mode.
The surgeon attempts to locate and dissect the cystic duct, pointing out its presumed location within the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters difficulty on the medial side of the gallbladder and decides to return to the lateral side for dissection, where the exposure is better and the procedure progresses more smoothly.
The surgeon flips the gallbladder to create a window behind it, freeing the tissue up to the top of the gallbladder, facilitating the establishment of the critical view.
The surgeon focuses on the junction between the cystic duct and the gallbladder, attempting to isolate and define this anatomical area.
The surgeon attempts to create a window between the cystic duct and the artery, which are closely positioned. Manipulation of the gallbladder is challenging due to the proximity of the artery.
The surgeon employs a top-down approach to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, improving exposure of the cystic artery situated above the cystic duct.
The surgeon completes the top-down dissection of the gallbladder, ensuring it is fully detached from the liver bed, providing clear exposure of the cystic artery and duct.
The surgeon approaches the cystic artery and cystic duct complex, carefully freeing the surrounding adventitial tissue to expose the junctional point between these structures.
The surgeon visually identifies the junction of the cystic duct with the common bile duct, highlighting the anatomical connection between these structures.
The surgeon attempts to free the cystic artery from surrounding tissue to prepare for clip placement, focusing on the area where the artery runs adjacent to the cystic duct.
The surgeon addresses bleeding from the cystic artery on the specimen side, maintaining control while cleaning the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a window beneath the cystic artery and duct complex, addressing bleeding with focused bipolar cautery while maintaining good exposure from prior mobilization.
The surgeon completes the creation of a window under the cystic artery and applies clips to secure the vessel.
The surgeon thins out the tissue surrounding the cystic duct, shaping it into a funnel to facilitate the placement of clips. The cystic duct is observed to be unusually wide in this patient.
The surgeon assesses the cystic duct, noting its thinning as it approaches the junction with the common bile duct, making it suitable for applying Hem-o-lok clips.
Otherwise I probably would have put a free tie and then sutured it.

The surgeon continues to thin out the tissue around the cystic duct for clip placement, achieving a clear critical view. The cystic duct is distinctly visible alongside the common bile duct and the common hepatic duct.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and then transects it, completing the separation of the duct from surrounding structures.
Case overall went very well. Took just about one hour start to finish. I was very pleased. And my arms and my fingers didn't hurt when I was done. I hope you enjoyed.
